question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) , given the falling number of trout returning to spawn in rivers from Lough Neagh, whether he will issue a section 14 permit for the hatchery in Kells to collect broodstock dollaghan trout; and (ii) to detail the reasons for any proposed approach in relation to this matter.
answer_text: (i)	My Department currently has no plans to issue a Section 14 permit for the hatchery in Kells to collect broodstock dollaghan trout.(ii)	Restocking in itself does not address any causes that may be limiting fish stock numbers, and indeed, scientific studies suggest that while hatcheries may help stock rebuilding, they are unsuitable for the long-term sustainability of fish populations. Genetic studies carried out reveal a complex micro-genetic structure in trout populations across Lough Neagh tributaries, which needs to be preserved. Stocking hatchery-bred fry could harm wild fish populations with little long-term positive impact on trout populations. Fishery management efforts should focus on protecting water quality and enhancing habitats to conserve to restore native fish populations. Angling clubs are therefore encouraged to consider these options first in an effort to increase native wild fish stocks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of fishing licences issued; and (ii) the revenue raised from their sale, in each of the last five years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the financial cost of the cull of 28,500 trout at Movanagher Fish Farm; and (ii) the estimated cost of a further cull of 100,000 trout at Movanagher Fish Farm.
answer_text: Limited culls of 28,250 fish in November 2023 and of 44,351 fish in June 2024 have been conducted. My Department does not consider a further fish cull necessary at this time. These culls were the minimum number necessary to provide additional space for the welfare of fish remaining on the farm until a treatment protocol for zebra mussels, an invasive species, can be developed.  This will allow recommencement of stocking while complying with the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985. Without intervention, a significant number would have died due to overcrowding, cannibalism, and disease outbreaks. The culls were authorised and overseen by a veterinarian surgeon.DateNumber of fishLoss (value of fish)Cost of CullNov-2328,250£ 74,591.17£   8,164Jun-2444,351£ 70,849.06£ 10,203
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) who will be responsible for enforcing the new regulations that will come into force on 5 July 2024 in relation to XL Bully type dogs; and (ii) what additional resources have been provided to deliver the additional processes.
answer_text: Council dog wardens are responsible for enforcing dog control legislation in Northern Ireland, including the new restrictions on owning XL Bully type dogs. I am committed to providing additional resource to councils, to support training for dog wardens, update IT systems and fund other associated requirements.  My Department continues to meet regularly with Northern Ireland Dog Advisory Group, which represents council dog wardens, on the implementation of the exemption scheme and to determine additional costs to councils.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 12834/22-27, to detail how the information provided in the answer, that valuable field data and deployment experience was gained through the project results, it was not intended to evidence the effectiveness of TVR as a means of reducing TB in cattle, is in keeping with information on his Department's website, that officials considered that a TVR study would increase the evidence base around badgers and bovine TB and help create well-informed and evidence-based strategies to address the issue of cattle-to-cattle
answer_text: As previously advised, the Test, Vaccine or Remove (TVR) research project achieved its objective of providing valuable field data and experience of deploying such an intervention.  For instance, the TVR project clearly demonstrated that cage side testing of badgers for Mycobacterium bovis (M. bovis) infection was a practical option enabling real time decision making of the fate of each badger e.g. removal or vaccinate and release.  The data collected also played a major role in the validation of the new test for badgers (Dual-Path Platform VetTB test, usually abbreviated to DPP test) that replaced the previously validated test (Brock TB Stat-Pak test).The project also provided accurate information on the resources that would be required to carry out a badger intervention project over a 100km2 area, particularly in terms of staffing and equipment.  This provided valuable data that were utilised in the business case developed to explore different badger intervention options. Staff participating in the project developed a wealth of knowledge relating to badgers and their ecology.  Field staff became experienced at recognising evidence of badger activity and adept at carrying out badger sett surveys.  These skills were passed onto other staff.With regards to the statement that the project ‘was not intended to evidence the effectiveness of TVR as a means of reducing TB in cattle’, prior to the commencement of the TVR project, a calculation was carried out that showed that the study would be underpowered to show evidence of a reduction in cattle herd tuberculosis infection levels (unless the effect was very large).However, the project did succeed in terms of increasing the evidence base around badgers and bovine TB, and in seeking to help create well-informed and evidence-based strategies to address the issue of cattle-to-cattle spread and badger-to-cattle spread.  My Department has, for many years, recommended that herd keepers should fence off badger setts on their land to prevent cattle accessing the setts.  One of the studies undertaken in the TVR area clearly showed that most interaction between cattle and the badger environment occurred at the badger sett entrances.  This was also shown through anecdotal evidence observed by field staff during their field activities.  This reinforced the importance for such biosecurity advice to herd keepers.The systematic sampling of badgers within the TVR area also showed that badgers did have a M. bovis infection prevalence of 19% at the start of the project, which is indicative that badgers can act as a maintenance host for the disease.   This was further evidenced by the M. bovis genomic study carried out on M. bovis isolates from badgers during the project.  The genomic study also indicated that badgers appeared to play a smaller role in the transmission dynamics of M. bovis than cattle, which is consistent with the view that cattle are the main multiplier host of the infection.  However, this minor role of
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given the ongoing cost of living crisis, to detail the steps the Executive is taking to alleviate poverty.
answer_text: This question has been passed to me to answer on behalf of the First Minister and Deputy First Minister as my Department is leading on development of the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy.Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me and I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for our communities right across Northern Ireland through the implementation of a robust Anti-poverty Strategy. However, it’s important to state that, in the absence of an agreed Strategy, work is being taken forward to support the most vulnerable in our society.For example, those who need to, can avail of Universal Credit, a benefit that includes support for the cost of housing, children and childcare, and financial support for people with disabilities, carers and people too ill to work.Cost of Living Payments were delivered throughout 2023/24 with a final payment of £299 to eligible people in receipt of an income-related benefit paid in February 2024 and in 2022/23, my Department’s Make the Call Wraparound service assisted over eleven and a half thousand people in accessing almost £55 million in additional benefits.My Department also provides financial support through the Discretionary Support Scheme that can offer help towards short-term living expenses or with the purchase of household items for those people in an extreme, exceptional or crisis situation.  Depending on personal circumstances applicants could be offered either an interest-free loan or a non-repayable grant.From April 2024 benefits  were increased by 6.7%, in line with the September 2023 rate of inflation, whilst the state pension was increased by 8.5%.My Department also provides help through the Affordable Warmth scheme, as well as the Winter Fuel Payments to those who are eligible. Funding has been allocated to our Social Supermarket Scheme to help those in or at risk of food poverty. Importantly, my Department also promotes the value of work, and the positive impact it can have on poverty, health and wellbeing, social inclusion, and our economy.	While my Department is taking this work forward, final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be a matter for the Executive.
department: Department for Communities

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his direction to officials to reallocate resources within his Department to ensure prioritisation of efforts to address environmental problems in Lough Neagh, including (i) how much has been identified to date; and (ii) where the resources are being allocated from within his budget.
answer_text: Since Executive approval of Budget 2024-25 I have had to take difficult decisions to help manage rising statutory obligation costs such as Bovine TB Compensation and other inflationary contractual pressures on pay and operational costs as well as new additional work in relation to Climate Change and Lough Neagh within the reduced budget.  Currently I have allocated £2.7 million Resource DEL to take forward additional work in relation to Lough Neagh.  This has been reallocated from savings in staff costs budgets including not replacing leavers, overtime and agency costs as well as in general administration expenditure.  I have also allocated £3.2m Capital DEL for work in relation to Lough Neagh.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 12691/22-27 and AQW 12692/22-27, for (i) his assessment of whether a clear evidential need has been identified; and (ii) his assessment of any evidence provided by local councils in relation to a register of animal welfare offenders.
answer_text: In considering an operational register data concerning reduction in animal welfare offending or reoffending is considered as part of evidential need. 		Whilst the current assessment is that a register is unlikely to be effectual or demonstrate value for money due to a range of operational challenges associated with implementation and operation of a register I continue to seek further information where a register has worked effectively elsewhere as per AQW 12691/22-27 and have stated to my officials that I wish to review the current evidence base in due course.  		I also intend to write to my counterpart in the Irish Government, to seek their views on a register and if one could be created on an all-island basis. 		I am very conscious of stakeholder views and I wish to restate my commitment to engaging with these organisations in the time ahead, as I determine the direction for animal welfare policy and legislation.  		I am also content to meet the member to discuss the matters associated with a potential register if desired.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how he is using the £1.6 million capital funding allocated to address problems associated with Lough Neagh; (ii) what it will be spent on; and (iii) when work will (a) commence; and (b) be completed.
answer_text: A Capital budgetary allocation of £1.5m has been allocated to Lough Neagh actions. This will be utilised in additional monitoring within the Lough Neagh catchment and the Lough itself. Work has already commenced on the identification of monitoring requirements to provide almost real time monitoring of nutrient contributions of inflowing rivers and within the lough itself and will be followed by procurement of suitable equipment. It is anticipated that procurement of monitoring equipment will be completed within this financial year and implementation of enhanced monitoring being implemented over the next three years.The funding will also be utilised to commence work on the development of IT systems to improve how my Department captures and interprets data to enable the identification of priority areas for regulation and enforcement.  This will include sources and loading of nutrients and their relationship to water quality, thereby supporting implementation of recommendations within the Lough Neagh Report.  The scoping and preliminary work on IT systems will be undertaken in this financial year with development following over the next 3 years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the test and vaccinate or remove scheme that was piloted by his Department as a method of removing bovine TB from the wildlife population.
answer_text: The Test and Vaccinate or Remove (TVR) Research Project was carried out over five years between 2014 and 2018 and encompassed an area of approximately 100 square kilometres near Banbridge in County Down. The project sought to capture badgers, test them for TB, and then vaccinate those with negative results or remove those with positive results following a cage side test. During the project, 824 unique badgers were captured, some of which were captured and tested more than once. 1,520 badger captures were made in total,  146 of which tested positive for bovine TB at the sett-side Dual Path Platform blood test. Over the course of the project, 781 unique badgers were vaccinated at least once while 108 infected badgers were euthanised in years 2 to 5. A low level of badger removal was required (4.1%–16.4% of captured badgers annually).  Analysis of the data indicated a 39% annual reduction in badger infection prevalence over the five years (from a starting prevalence of 19% to 2% in year 5).The data indicated that the intervention had little effect on the badger population size. Combined with GPS recordings and badger genotyping this also consistently showed no increased movement/perturbation of badgers, indicating that trap-side testing was a viable intervention option. Whilst valuable field data and deployment experience was gained through the project results, it was not intended to evidence the effectiveness of TVR as a means of reducing TB in cattle. The findings of the TVR research project have been published in a number of separate papers.  Each of the published papers addresses specific aspects of the project including the interaction between cattle and badgers, and how these interactions may affect the spread of bovine TB.I will place an updated list of TVR papers published to date in the library of the House.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the level of water quality monitoring that his Department routinely conducts at Lough Neagh.
answer_text: The Water Quality (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017, (WFD Regulations), identify the quality elements that should be monitored in rivers and lakes and the frequencies at which this monitoring should be carried out. Classification tools have been developed and standards set for various quality elements within the Regulations. Physicochemical sampling takes place monthly at the inflowing rivers and at the outflow from Lough Neagh at Toome.  Biological surveys are conducted within Lough Neagh, twice in the 6-year cycle at 15 sites around the lough, and phytoplankton is sampled at the outflow during the summer months. In the Lough Neagh catchment, biological monitoring is carried out at the eight inflowing rivers and the outflowing Lower Bann river, on a rolling programme during the 6-year WFD cycle.   Rivers are sampled once or twice per WFD cycle for invertebrates, 4 samples per cycle for diatoms and once every cycle for macrophytes.  An additional monitoring program for benthic invertebrates, outside of WFD requirements, was re-established by DAERA in 2023/24. This joins with a long-term monitoring programme of temperature and dissolved oxygen for the Lough and the six large rivers feeding into the Lough. Fish surveys in Lough Neagh are carried out annually by AFBI colleagues.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how often a TB reactor animal has not been removed from its herd within the recommended 15 working day timeframe in each month of the last three years.
answer_text: My Department aims to remove bovine tuberculosis (bTB) reactors off farm as quickly as possible to minimise the ongoing disease risk posed by these animals. The current DAERA target for reactor removal is that 90% of TB reactors are removed within 15 working days of test completion. The 15 working days are necessary to allow time for all steps of the removal procedure to be carried out.  The main steps include submission of the bTB test results; interpretation of the test results; creation of the valuation request; arrangement and completion of the valuation; submission of the valuation; creation of the movement licence; and removal of the reactors for slaughter.I have attached a table at Annex A which provides the monthly figures for reactors removed, the number that missed the 15 working day target and percentage that achieved the 15 working day target over the last three years. Factors which have contributed to the missed target since January 2023, include an increase in the numbers of cattle to be valued and removed, staff shortages within the Valuation Unit, industrial action in October 2023 and the launch of NIFAIS in September 2023.  It is hoped that a recent recruitment competition in the Valuation Unit will help ensure that we meet our targeted 90% of TB reactors removed within 15 working days of test completion.Other common reasons for a missed target include cows in the later stages of pregnancy and lame cattle being held on farm.  Under welfare legislation, pregnant animals cannot be transported in the last 10% of gestation or within a week post calving which can result in a delay to slaughter if they are reactor animals. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the level of water quality monitoring that his Department routinely conducts at Lough Neagh.
answer_text: The NI Environment Agency (NIEA) is the lead organisation responsible for water quality monitoring in Lough Neagh including drinking water quality. However, in respect of drinking water quality, NI Water undertakes sampling and analysis of the raw water abstracted from Lough Neagh in line with the Water Supply (Water Quality) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017.
department: Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 5733/22-27, whether his Department has considered the establishment of a Northern Ireland-specific register.
answer_text: My department considered if a register of animal welfare offenders could be established in Northern Ireland and on an all-island basis. The situation remains that a clear evidential need has not been identified, the costs of establishing and maintaining a register would be significant and the data protection implications involved are prohibitive. 		However, if Assembly colleagues or stakeholders advocating for a register can provide additional evidence to support the establishment and operation of a register, which could include where any such register has worked effectively elsewhere, my officials will consider any such detail received.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how much of her current budget allocation, in both value and percentage, has been set aside for pay increases for 2024-25 for (i) the PSNI; and (ii) the Prison Service.
answer_text: The budget currently available has been allocated in full to business areas across my Department including allocations to both (i) the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) and (ii) the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS). In allocating budgets to business areas, funding has not been earmarked for pay increases. It will be for each of these organisations to manage their pressures, including pay-related pressures, and prioritise their budget allocations accordingly. It should be also be noted that no decisions have been taken yet in relation to the level of pay awards for 2024-25 in either the PSNI or NIPS.
department: Department of Justice

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the financial support his Department has provided for the agricultural shows in (i) Ballymoney; (ii) Ballymena; and (iii) Antrim in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In the period 2019 to 2023 my department provided a total of £29,349.45 in financial support to agricultural shows in Ballymoney, Ballymena and Antrim. It will be useful to note that these agricultural shows were cancelled in 2020 and 2021 due to the Covid-19 pandemic. An itemised breakdown of the support provided over the past five years is set out in Table 1 below.Table 1Financial support provided to Ballymoney, Ballymena and Antrim Agricultural Shows respectively, 2019 to 2023, administered via sponsorship payments and/or Northern Ireland Regional Food Programme (NIRFP) funding.BallymoneyBallymenaAntrim2019Sponsorship£300.00£250.00£250.00NIRFP£1,640.45£0.00£1,965.002020Sponsorship£0.00£0.00£0.00NIRFP£0.00£0.00£0.002021Sponsorship£0.00£0.00£0.00NIRFP£0.00£0.00£0.002022Sponsorship£0.00£0.00£0.00NIRFP£2,975.66£0.00£0.00Bounce Back Support Scheme£9,864.40£2,053.54£6,966.402023Sponsorship£0.00£0.00£0.00NIRFP£3,084.00£0.00£0.00Total financial support provided£17,864.51£2,303.54£9,181.40
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions he has taken to prevent illegal fishing in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: My Department has a dedicated Lough Neagh fisheries enforcement team which carry out a programme of regular patrols, inspections of fishing gear and checks on fish dealers to ensure compliance with legislation. Private Water Bailiffs also patrol Lough Neagh and have the same powers to inspect fishing gear and take prosecutions for illegal fishing.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
